questions.question: What are the two jobs of Week 4?

options.text: Keep executing and start documenting sprint evidence
Plan the next sprint and hire help
Focus only on closing existing deals

questions.explanation: Week 4 has two jobs: keep executing at the same volume as Week 3 (don't coast) and start documenting your sprint evidence for your certification portfolio. The leads generated in Week 4 build the pipeline for the month after.

questions.question: What should be included in the Sprint ROI calculation?

options.text: Only tool costs and ad spend
Time cost (hours x hourly rate), tool costs, and total revenue return (closed + probability-weighted pipeline)

questions.explanation: Sprint ROI includes total investment (time cost at your hourly rate + tool costs) divided into total return (closed revenue + probability-weighted pipeline value). This tells you the actual ROI per hour invested in acquisition activities.

questions.question: After the sprint ends, what should become permanent weekly habits?

options.text: Sustainable outreach volume, weekly pipeline review, CRM hygiene, and systematic referral asks

questions.explanation: Some sprint activities become permanent habits: sustainable outreach volume, weekly pipeline reviews, 15-minute CRM hygiene sweeps, and referral asks at customer milestones. The sprint was a burst; the steady-state system continues.

questions.question: The most important artifact of the sprint is the revenue generated.
questions.explanation: False. The most important artifact is what you now know that you didn't know before — which channels work, which message variants convert, how long the sales cycle really is, and what your actual conversion rates are. Knowledge compounds; a single deal doesn't.

questions.question: Summarize what you would expect to learn from a 30-day sprint. What are the top 3 things that would 'work' and the top 3 things that might 'not work'? What single biggest insight would you hope to gain?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response demonstrates understanding of the sprint as a learning exercise, identifies realistic wins and failures, and articulates a single compelling insight about their acquisition system (e.g., pipeline lag, channel ROI differences, or the compounding effect of consistent execution).
